# Chapter 02 - Chapter 2

## Draft Paragraphs (keyed)

[[CH02-P0010]] False dawn seeped through the dome’s glass as Marnette found the pulse at Master Frost’s wrist. His skin felt cool, papery under her fingertips, the blue of his veins too visible, too close to the surface. She counted against her watch. Seventy-two. Weak, but steady. His breathing had eased overnight, the rattle subdued to a faint roughness that did not quite disappear. Another day, then. She told herself it was a simple fact, a small success of the body. Still, she could not help wondering what survival meant to a man who had outlasted centuries. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0020]] "Good morning, Master Frost," Marnette said softly, easing the blanket back up where it had slipped from his chest. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0030]] His eyes opened and settled on her with the same disquieting clarity as yesterday, pale blue and fully present. "Marnette. Did you sleep well?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0040]] "I did, thank you." Marnette moved to the IV stand and checked the fluid level, then adjusted the drip to match the physician’s schedule. "Your vital signs stabilised during the night. The pain relief is holding better now." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0050]] He watched her hands as they moved, practised and precise. "You’re not afraid to touch me." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0060]] Marnette paused with her fingertips resting on the ventilator controls. "Should I be?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0070]] "Most people are. They think they’ll break me, or catch death like a disease." He tried to shift, pain tightening his face; he gave up and sank back into the pillows. "Or perhaps they’re simply afraid of their own dying." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0080]] "Dying is part of Elom’s plan," she said. The words came automatically, but she meant them. "My role is to make your passage comfortable, not to fear it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0090]] She washed her hands at the small basin, letting the warm water steady her. Morning care had a rhythm she trusted, each task a way to give the body dignity when it was beginning to fail. Vitals first, then medication timings. Then gentle range-of-motion work to slow the wasting, slow bends and stretches of arms and legs, his body light and fragile in her hands. After that came washing, drying, and fresh nightclothes. The fabric was finer than anything in the salve wards, soft against her work-rough fingers, and the contrast made her careful not to show what she noticed. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0100]] "Your grandmother died at fifty-three," Frost said abruptly as Marnette eased him higher for his morning meal. "It’s in your service record. I read it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0110]] Marnette went still for a beat, then kept her hands moving, smoothing and arranging the pillows as if nothing had shifted. "Yes," she said. "She lived a good long life." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0120]] "Fifty-three years," Frost said. "Did it feel like a long life to you?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0130]] "She raised me and my two brothers after our parents died," Marnette said, surprised by how quickly the details lined up, as if her mind kept them ready. "She worked in the agricultural domes for forty-two years. She lived through the colony’s growth from three habitats to twelve. She prayed every morning and never missed a service." She paused, then met his gaze because evasion would only invite more. "Yes. I thought it was long. I thought it was full." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0140]] "What did she die of?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0150]] "Cellular breakdown," Marnette said. "The same as most salves who reach their full years." She lifted the nutrient bowl from the bedside table, thick and pale green, fortified for a Master’s failing system. "It was peaceful. She was ready." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0160]] Frost ate slowly, each swallow a visible effort in his thin throat. Marnette steadied him with one hand at his back, keeping him upright and safe between breaths. He might have been surprised by her strength, but it was ordinary to her. She had lifted and turned bodies twice his weight, year after year, trained by necessity to become a kind of quiet machinery for people who could no longer hold themselves up. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0170]] "Do you ever wonder what she would have been like at sixty," he asked between spoonfuls, "or seventy?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0180]] "Why would I?" Marnette said. "Elom gave her fifty-three years. That was her time, her purpose." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0190]] "But do you not see her sometimes," he pressed, "in dreams perhaps, older than she ever had the chance to become?" His spoon shook, and Marnette steadied it without thinking. "My mother lived to nine hundred and ninety-two. I remember her at seven hundred, eight hundred, her face lined, her hands still steady enough to tend a garden. Do you have memories like that of your grandmother, Marnette. Or were you never allowed the years for them to exist?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0200]] Marnette set the empty bowl down with careful precision. "Master Frost, you speak like someone with too much time to sit with old questions. My grandmother was content. She was faithful. She died knowing she had served Elom well." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0210]] "I’m not questioning her contentment," he said, and his voice softened, almost careful. "I’m questioning the container." His pale eyes stayed on her. "Does it never strike you as strange that your containers all hold the same measure of time. Fifty years, sixty, rarely more. As if they were manufactured to a specification." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0220]] Manufactured. The word hung in the air, too practical, too human. He was old and dying, she told herself, and minds wandered at the end. She smoothed his blanket as if fabric could press the moment flat. "The window is clear today," she said. "You can see the mountains from here. They look beautiful in this light." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0230]] He allowed the redirection, his gaze travelling to the wide windows. Beyond the dome, Mars lay open and bare, red distance under the habitat’s managed sky. A mountain range rose on the horizon, peaks cut clean against the thin atmosphere, ancient volcanoes long since spent. Marnette looked at them and felt time in her bones, the scale of it, the way a world could burn and cool and remain indifferent. Humans had come later with their domes and their vows, and called it living. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0240]] "They say the first colonists named them after old gods," Frost said, his voice going distant. "Olympus. Ascraeus. As if naming could turn rock into meaning." His eyes stayed on the horizon. "Perhaps they understood that to survive here, people needed stories. Something larger than themselves to hold on to." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0250]] Marnette returned to her tasks. She checked the medication timings, logged his vital signs on the datapad, and adjusted the room temperature to the setting noted in his chart. Procedure steadied her. The work had edges she could hold, numbers she could trust. She knew this part of herself, competent and clear. His questions could be set aside as symptoms, as eccentricity, as the mind’s stray sparks at the end. His condition was stable for the moment. Her duty, at least, was not in question. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0260]] "I have the evening off tonight," she said as she gathered the breakfast tray. "There’s a service at seventh hour. Would you like me to read to you before I go?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0270]] "The service," he repeated. "The Church of Elom." His gaze caught hers again, too intent for a casual question. "Tell me about your faith, Marnette. What do they teach you there?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0280]] The question wrong-footed her. Not the subject, Masters sometimes asked about salve beliefs with a distant sort of curiosity, but the force behind it. Frost leaned forward despite the pain it plainly cost him, as if the answer mattered enough to spend what little strength he had left. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0290]] "They teach us that Elom designed us for purpose," Marnette said, keeping her voice level. "That each of us has a role in the colony’s survival, in Elom’s design. They teach us our lives are brief, but sacred, because they serve something larger than ourselves." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0300]] "Something larger than yourselves," he repeated, as if testing the phrase for weakness. "And what, exactly, is that. The colony? The continuation of the species?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0310]] "The continuation of Elom’s plan," Marnette said, gently, because correction could be its own kind of insolence. "We’re each part of a tapestry. Some threads run longer than others, but each has its place, its necessity." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0320]] "A tapestry," he echoed, and settled back into the pillows as if the words had spent him. "Go to your service, Marnette. Listen to what you are told. Then think about what you believe, not only what you’ve been taught." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0330]] "That’s the same thing," Marnette said, and heard her own confusion in it. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0340]] "Is it?" He let his eyes close. "We’ll talk when you return." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0350]] The ride back to the salve district took thirty-seven minutes through the connecting tubes. Marnette watched the colony change in layers as they passed, Master residences thinning into neutral corridors and then into the functional blocks where salves lived and worked. Even the light altered. It flattened into uniform illumination, reliable and pale, with none of the depth and shadow she had seen in Frost’s rooms. The shift should have been comforting. Instead it made her feel how quickly she had adjusted to the other kind of brightness. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0360]] As she walked towards the Church of Elom, something in her loosened, then settled again. This was her world. Her people. Her place. Red dust film on every ledge, the muted palette of salve tunics, the quiet order of bodies moving with purpose through narrow corridors. Here, nothing glittered, nothing pretended to be effortless. It felt real in a way Frost’s rooms could not, however beautiful they were. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0370]] The Church of Elom was set into the habitat wall, its ceiling curving up towards the dome like a palm turned to receive light. The space was humble, but carefully made. Mosaics ran along the walls in scenes from the colony’s founding: the first colonist looking up at the red planet, the first habitat frames lifting into place, the first children presented as if purpose arrived with breath. There was no gold leaf, no ostentatious display. Everything here was functional and symbolic, organised to instruct, to steady, to inspire. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0380]] Lena was there when Marnette arrived, seated near the front as always. She lifted a hand and patted the space beside her, claiming it with the easy certainty of friendship. Lena worked hydroponics, hands often stained faint green from nutrient solution, nails never quite clean. Her cheer was steady too, not naïve so much as chosen, a kind of defiance against exhaustion. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0390]] "You look tired," Lena murmured as Marnette sat. "Is Master Frost keeping you busy?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0400]] "He has his moments." Marnette smoothed her grey tunic and checked, by habit, that her prayer beads sat properly beneath the collar. "Mostly he sleeps. Or he asks questions that don’t sound like anything I’ve heard before." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0410]] "Aren’t all Masters strange?" Lena’s mouth curved, but the humour faded almost at once. "How is he, really?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0420]] "Dying," Marnette said, and the word felt heavier here, among her own. "And he knows it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0430]] Priest Malachi approached the altar, his white robes stark against the plain wood. He was older than most salves, fifty-seven if Marnette remembered correctly, old enough that some people spoke of his birth as if it belonged to a different era. His hair had gone silver and his face carried the lines of long service. When he lifted his hands to begin, the congregation stilled, silence settling like a held breath. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0440]] "Elom’s children," he began, his voice filling the modest space without strain. "We gather again in gratitude and purpose, in acceptance of our sacred design." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0450]] The opening prayer was one Marnette had carried since childhood, its cadence stitched into her breathing. "Elom, creator and guide, who designed us for purpose, who gave each of us our role in the colony, who sees and knows, help us to serve with devotion. Help us to accept with grace. Help us to find joy in our designated time." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0460]] Marnette joined the chorus, her voice sliding into the shared cadence until she could not tell where she ended and the congregation began. The collective sound steadied her, made her feel held. This was what she believed. This was what had carried her through exhaustion and grief. Frost’s questions were strange, unsettling, but here, in the familiar words, truth still felt solid under her feet. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0470]] Priest Malachi’s sermon that evening was on the beauty of brief lives. "Some look at the Masters," he said, his voice warming as it rose, "and see only longevity. They see hundreds of years and think that is blessing. Elom teaches us otherwise. Blessing is not measured in time, but in impact, in purpose, in devotion." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0480]] Heads bowed and rose in unison, a soft murmur of agreement travelling through the pews. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0490]] "A salve who lives fifty years and serves faithfully, who raises children in the faith, who keeps the colony alive with labour and care, lives a life of greater meaning than a Master who spends eight hundred years in idle luxury," Malachi said. "Why? Because your lives are focused. Your time is precious. Every moment matters because it is limited." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0500]] Tears stung at Marnette’s eyes before she could stop them. Yes, she thought, relieved by the certainty. This was the comfort she relied on, the meaning that kept her moving when her body wanted to give in. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0510]] "Some ask why," Priest Malachi continued, and his voice lowered, drawing the room closer. "Some look at our limited years and question Elom’s wisdom. Understand this. Elom did not limit you. He optimised you." The word landed with a strange weight. "He designed you for efficiency, for impact. A life of fifty years devoted to purpose is of greater value to Elom’s plan than centuries spent in leisure." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0520]] He paused and looked out over them, letting the silence do its work. "When doubt comes, remember this. You are not less than the Masters. You are different. You are specialised. You are essential." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0530]] Marnette’s fingers closed around her prayer beads until the smooth spheres pressed into her skin. Essential. That was what she was. What they all were. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0540]] After the service, while people lingered in small knots of conversation, Lena caught Marnette by the arm. "Walk with me," she said. "I need to talk about Thomas." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0550]] Thomas was Lena’s younger brother, nineteen, newly assigned to maintenance in the industrial sector. Marnette felt a small tightening of concern before she could stop it. "Is he having trouble at work?" she asked as they stepped out into the corridor. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0560]] "Not exactly." Lena lowered her voice. "He’s been talking to some of the older workers. The ones who were around before, you know." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0570]] Marnette nodded. Before the Great Optimisation twenty years ago, when the Church had tightened its teachings, when certain questions were said to be settled for good and certain doubts quietly disappeared. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0580]] "Thomas keeps asking why," Lena said. "Why some salves can’t live longer. Why some die in their forties while others reach sixty. He says it isn’t fair." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0590]] "Fair has nothing to do with it," Marnette said, and heard how odd the word sounded on her tongue. "Elom’s design isn’t about fairness. It’s about purpose." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0600]] "That’s what I told him. But he keeps pointing to the pre-Optimisation records, the ones they released. He says there was more variation before." Lena shook her head, worry tightening her mouth. "I’m frightened for him. Doubt is like rust. It spreads." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0610]] "Doubt is dangerous," Marnette said, and felt Frost’s questions stir in the back of her mind like grit caught under skin. She pushed them down. "Pray for him. Bring him to Priest Malachi." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0620]] "I will." Lena hesitated, then said, "But Marnette, be careful with this assignment. Some Masters, when they’re dying, get strange ideas. They forget their place, or they start testing yours." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0630]] "It is strange," Marnette said, keeping her voice low. "He asked me the same thing this morning. Whether it ever struck me as strange that we all die at roughly the same age." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0640]] "And what did you say?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0650]] "That we don’t all die at the same age," Marnette said. "We live to our designated time. And Elom’s design is perfect." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0660]] Lena nodded, relief loosening her shoulders. "Good. Just remember who you are. Remember what you believe." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0670]] The ride back to Residence 7B did not feel the same as it had yesterday. Marnette watched the Master sector slide past and felt something shift behind her ribs, not awe exactly, not even envy. Something sharper. What did it do to a person to live for centuries, to watch generations rise and vanish like breath on glass. What did it mean to stand so close to the beginning that the founder’s presence would be memory rather than myth. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0680]] Elara was waiting at the door. "Master Frost had pain while you were gone," she said. "He asked for you. Specifically." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0690]] "Is he stable now?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0700]] "For now. The physician adjusted his medication. He may be confused." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0710]] Marnette entered Frost’s chambers without sound. The lights had been lowered for evening, leaving the room in a soft wash of shadow. He lay propped against the pillows, face pale and damp with sweat, the effort of his own body written in the shine at his temples. Yet his eyes were clear. They found her as she approached and held fast, too focused to be dismissed as confusion. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0720]] "You went to the service," he said, his voice weaker than this morning. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0730]] "I did. Priest Malachi spoke about the beauty of brief lives." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0740]] Frost’s mouth moved in the faintest hint of a smile. "Did he. And what did he say?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0750]] "He said our lives are precious because they’re focused," Marnette said. She smoothed Frost’s blanket, checking his comfort as she spoke, letting habit keep her hands steady. "That we’re optimised for purpose, not limited by it. That being specialised is better than being general." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0760]] "Optimised for purpose," Frost repeated. "An interesting way to put it. And what purpose are you optimised for, Marnette?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0770]] "Caring for others," Marnette said. "Serving those who can’t serve themselves." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0780]] "And when you’re forty," he said, "or fifty. When your hands begin to shake, when your back aches from lifting bodies twice your weight. What then?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0790]] "Then I’ll train younger caregivers," Marnette said. "I’ll pass on what I know." She hesitated, hearing the catch in her own certainty. "If I live that long. My grandmother died at fifty-three." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0800]] "Because her cells broke down. Because that is what they were designed to do." Frost’s voice strengthened on the last words, as if pain could be turned into emphasis. He shifted, face tightening, and tried again. "Help me sit up. Higher." He drew a careful breath. "There’s something I need to show you." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0810]] Marnette shifted the pillows and braced his back as he forced himself higher. The effort stole his breath. His skin blanched further, the sweat on his temples bright in the low light. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0820]] "By the window," he said. "On the table. The black folder." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0830]] She crossed to the window-side table, her steps quiet on the polished floor. The folder lay where he said it would, black and unmarked, its surface like leather to the touch but weighted like something reinforced. When she brought it back, Frost’s fingers shook as he worked at the clasp, stubborn with effort. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0840]] "Open it," he said. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0850]] The contents wrong-footed her. Not documents or slates, but photographs, real paper prints with worn edges, the kind of relic only Masters kept. People smiling, people celebrating, faces caught mid-laughter. Then she saw the sequence. The same woman across decades, her features changing in slow, honest increments: thirty, forty, fifty, then beyond what Marnette’s mind wanted to allow. Sixty. Seventy. The numbers rose in her head as if she were counting vital signs, and her throat tightened with the effort of staying calm. The woman looked like a salve. She looked like someone who should have stopped existing long before the last frame. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0860]] "These are from before," Frost said quietly. "Before the colony stabilised. Before the Church fixed its teachings. Before what they later called optimisation." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0870]] Marnette stared at the photographs until the paper blurred, then forced herself to look properly. The woman was happy in each frame, not staged happiness but something lived-in. In the last image her hair had gone silver and her face was lined, yet her eyes were bright, her smile unforced. She stood among family, children pressed close, older hands on shoulders, bodies leaning in with the easy intimacy of people who expected more years together. Grandchildren, Marnette thought, and the word landed like a bruise. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0880]] "Who is she?" Marnette asked, her voice almost too small to carry. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0890]] "Her name was Elena," Frost said. "One of the first salves born on Mars. She lived to eighty-seven." He took a shallow breath, the pause between words measured. "She died in her garden, tending tomato plants. Her heart failed, the physician wrote. Old age." His eyes did not leave Marnette’s face. "Not cellular breakdown. Not planned obsolescence." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0900]] Marnette could not stop looking. Elena at seventy with a grandchild tucked into the crook of her arm, both of them smiling as if time were plentiful. Elena at eighty, her face written over with joy and grief and ordinary living. Eighty-seven years. The number sat in Marnette’s mind like a stone. It was not a little longer. It was an entire second lifetime, compared with what salves were allowed now. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0910]] "Marnette," Frost said, and his voice sharpened with a strength that did not match his body. "Look at me." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0920]] Marnette made herself lift her eyes to his. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0930]] "What if I told you your grandmother could have lived to seventy. Or eighty." Frost’s words came steady, each one placed like a weight. "What if I told you fifty years is not a design feature, it is a limitation. What if I told you the Great Optimisation twenty years ago was not about clarifying teachings. It was about putting biological controls in place, controls planned long before you were born." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0940]] The room seemed to chill, though the vents still breathed their regulated air. Marnette stepped back and the folder slid from her numb fingers, striking the floor with a soft thud. "Master Frost, you shouldn’t say that," she whispered. The words scraped out of her. "It’s dangerous." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0950]] "Dangerous to whom?" Frost asked. "To the Masters who need a compliant workforce. To a system that depends on salves dying before they can accumulate knowledge, before they can gather power." He broke into a cough, wet and painful, and for a moment he could do nothing but fight for air. When it eased, he looked at her again. "Or dangerous to you, because once you see it, you cannot unsee it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0960]] "You’re dying," she said, and the words shook in her throat. "The pain medication is confusing you. It’s making you say things that aren’t true." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0970]] "Am I?" he said. "Or am I finally saying what’s true?" His hand twitched towards the photographs scattered on the floor. "That’s your history, Marnette. Not the cleaned-up version they teach in school. Not the religious explanation that keeps everyone obedient. The real history." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0980]] Marnette backed away until the door was at her back. "I need to check your medication schedule. I need to…" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P0990]] "You need to think," he said, and his voice rose with a sudden, rough urgency. "Think about why salve lifespans fall into such a narrow range. Think about why the Church of Elom was established alongside the genetic protocols. Think about why your prayers speak of a ‘designated time’ instead of a natural one." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1000]] He was breathing hard now, face flushed, and Marnette could not tell whether it was fever or fury. "Think about who benefits when you die at fifty-two instead of eighty-two. Think about who gains when your experience, your wisdom, your memories are cut short before they can gather into something dangerous." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1010]] "Stop," she whispered, and heard the plea in it. He kept going. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1020]] "Your Church teaches that you’re specialised, that you’re optimised," he said. "But optimised for what. For the colony’s survival, or for Masters’ comfort. For your own fulfilment, or for their control." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1030]] Marnette slipped out and pulled the door shut, then leaned against it, heart thudding hard enough to make her light-headed. The corridor was silent. The Master-sector lighting, meant to be soft, felt suddenly harsh, as if it could strip her down to whatever she was trying not to be. She wanted to run. To take the next transport back to the salve district and sink into the familiar comfort of her people, her faith, her old, unquestioning acceptance of her place. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1040]] But she was his caregiver. Duty was the only thing that still made sense. Elom guide me, she prayed, and felt the words turn thin inside her. Elom give me strength. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1050]] She took a slow breath, set her face, and went back in. Frost was watching her. His breathing rasped, but his eyes had not moved off her. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1060]] "Master Frost," she said, making her voice professional and keeping it that way. "It’s time for your evening medication. You need to rest." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1070]] He did not resist as she administered the drugs. He did not speak while she adjusted his pillows and pulled the blanket up. He simply watched her, and Marnette felt his gaze on every small, practiced motion. His expression was sad, almost gentle, as if he understood exactly what she was doing. Building walls. Retreating into the familiar safety of her role. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1080]] "Marnette," he said as she turned to leave. "What if I told you it wasn’t meant to be this way?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1090]] She paused at the door with her back to him. "What way, Master Frost?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1100]] "Any of it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1110]] She did not answer. She could not. The question went on sounding in the quiet behind her, then in her own head, turning familiar thoughts strange and leaving a cold, uncertain space where her certainty had been. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1120]] In her own room, Marnette knelt by the bed and took out her prayer beads. Their smoothness should have grounded her. She let them slide through her fingers anyway, clinging to the automatic rhythm as if it could pull her thoughts back into place. "Elom, creator and guide, who designed us for purpose, who gave each of us our role in the great colony…" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1130]] But the words felt different now. Not hollow, exactly, but unsettled. The meaning had not changed, yet the sound of it no longer soothed. Designed for purpose. Each of us our role. Designated time. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1140]] The phrases she had repeated a thousand times now carried Frost’s questions inside them. What if design meant engineering. What if purpose meant function. What if designated time meant planned obsolescence. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1150]] She rose from her knees and went to the window, looking out at the agricultural domes. Green life against red death. That was what she had always thought. Now she could not hold the word death in her mouth without tasting something else. Was it death at all, or simply termination. A thing switched off when its work was done, like equipment decommissioned and replaced. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1160]] The thought came unbidden, unwelcome, dangerous. Then Marnette saw it with sudden clarity. Frost had done exactly what he meant to do. He had planted doubt and left it to take root. It would grow in the dark of her mind, fed by his questions and the images he had scattered at her feet, nourished by the terrible possibility that her life, her faith, her whole sense of reality rested on something carefully constructed and false. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1170]] Marnette lay down and pulled the blanket up over her shoulders, as if it could keep the thoughts from finding her. She closed her eyes. Sleep would not come. Instead she saw the woman from the photographs, Elena, eighty-seven, dying in a garden with loved ones close enough to touch. Then she saw her grandmother, dead at fifty-three from cellular breakdown. The words no longer sounded like nature at work. They sounded like a failure planned in advance. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1180]] Frost’s pale blue eyes watched her from the dark behind her lids, steady and knowing. He understood exactly what he was doing to her faith, to her certainty, to the acceptance she had been taught to call sacred. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH02-P1190]] In the darkness of her assigned room, with red dust waiting beyond the habitat walls, Marnette understood she was no longer merely unsettled. She was beginning to question. Frost had been right about that much. Once you started, the questions only led one way, towards a truth that might be more terrible than any lie she had ever been taught. <!-- REV2 -->
